item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can't believe nobody guess this, but was this based on Stranger in a Strange Land?

(at one point in the story, the Martians form a committee to decide if Earth shall share the same fate as the former 5th planet that became the asteroid belt Between Mars and Jupiter. Oversimplified, but that's the gist of it…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this is related in some way to SCP-1050, but that's just a guess on my part

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved the writing style and the content in the SCP, but I feel I am missing something in the SCP, particularly in the last addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for space SCPs, and something tells me this might not have had humans at mind at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Precisely the same comment as Andariel, but I'll no-vote. It is still good writing, and I respect that way too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was about to melt with that sort of overwhelming Sagan/Tyson/Hawking/Kaku awe that you get, but then the addendum completely ruined it for me.

O5 COMMAND ONLY THE REAL DEAL NO BULLSHIT
– hide block

Just another "kill all humans! because they're all uppity and sassin' us, boy"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The main article I have found okay but not really gripping. The addendum, with its "killbots" implication, has had me cringe. Overall, I am afraid I will downvote this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is the date blacked out in an O5-clearance section of this file.

why

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Was I wrong to think that the message isn't from aliens at all? I thought it was some non-living intelligence that had always existed talking to others about whether they needed to eradicate life again, like it had sprouted up again like a fungus in their otherwise ordered universe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What does it for me is the end addendum.

To me it shows that it is neither for us, or about us.

There is something bigger at play, something dangerous and worrisome, and we aren't even a footnote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The council kinda makes me think of "have spacesuit will travel" but that seems a little off to me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was sort of expecting a warning against dem humans cuz they bad. Left enough mystery to keep me interested, involved the deep space probes, and implies a vast interstellar democracy reacting to a greater threat.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got the feeling reading this that it might somehow be associated with the "Destroyers" from SCP-1050.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is pretty interesting but I'm on the fence about the addendum because I don't think you should have to spell these things out because it ruins the wonder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The hot has ascended once again. It is time for us to act. You must choose now."

Don't you see? They're arguing over who has to get up and get the popcorn from the microwave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking "monolith" once I got to the message, and other discussion makes me think I'm correct in my assumption that 2001, 2010, 2061 and 3001 had at least something to do with this…

Other that that, this feels pretty 'meh'. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is not bad, but it's not particularly interesting to me either. I get from the discussion page that there might be some ambiguous 'vote now on destroying human life' element in there but the article didn't entertain me enough for a second reading. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a soft spot for space-based SCP articles but I was kind of on the fence about this one until I reread the addendum and looked at the "hot", "life" and "intelligence" bit again, at which point it occurred to me they maybe they weren't talking about us at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It isn't the "A Time Odyssey" series.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Please tell me it's not "A Time Odyssey" by Clarke and Baxter…

I was really disappointed in that one.

Your SCP article, on the other hand, is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, but thanks for playing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Close, but no cigar.

Right author. Wrong story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Were you inspired by a short story by Clarke short story, called Crusade?

Upvoted because I like that story, and something similar in SCP form is awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm aware of the difficulties in performing analysis on such distant objects, as well as the difficulties in translating a language with limited points of reference. If you want a further nit to pick, I'm surprised nobody picked up on the fact that the space probes in question are very ill equipped in order to detect electromagnetic radiation of this wavelength. I used an advanced, scientific technique known as "Artistic Licence" to get around these issues.

Having said that, I'm aware that significant uses of this may break suspension of disbelief. Should more people find this a problem, then I will elaborate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I totally don't buy that they managed to translate it from such a miniscule corpus.

Concur. Linguistically speaking, you need a massive amount of text side-by-side with the same message in a known language in order to get a valid translation. (There's a reason the message I wrote in SCP-1050 is so long!) The Rosetta Stone is quite long (the English translation is ~470 words, depending on the version of the translation), and up until it was discovered, nobody could read Egyptian hieroglyphs, despite the fact that they adorned countless monuments and artifacts throughout Egypt. It took having the same text side-by-side with Demotic (which had been translated) and lowest Ancient Greek (also translated). The reason it was significant historically is that the text in the other two languages was identified as being the same, which allowed Jean-François Champollion to deduce that the hieroglyphs were saying the same thing.

There are plenty of languages out there which have not yet been deciphered and translated, since no common reference has been discovered. Even if you go with the fact that this is supposed to be some binary or mathematically based language, you'd be presented the same problem. Example:

9999991837 2978049951 0597317328 1609631859 5024459455
3469083026 4252230825 3344685035 2619311881 7101000313
7838752886 5875332083 8142061717 7669147303 5982534904
2875546873 1159562863 8823537875 9375195778 1857780532
1712268066 1300192787 6611195909 2164201989 3809525720

Simply presented with this, it would be difficult to know what exactly is meant, unless you had some frame of reference. I've picked a set of numbers that has a pattern known to humans but with a single error to make it not Google-able. I admit I'm not a PhD in mathematics, but I've more than a little practice with cryptography, and I'd have trouble with this one. (Answer beneath the collapsible.)

+ show block
– hide block

It's digits 762-1101 digits of pi, starting at the Feynman point. The error is the digit "1" after the first six "9"s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seems a bit much of a generic space doom thing. Also, I totally don't buy that they managed to translate it from such a miniscule corpus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, we have at least one alien and there are hints that a few more are watching us, but there has yet to be a straight-up first contact between us and another alien clivilization. Well, you you know, if there was canon.

But the thing here is, these aliens may or may not be threatening to murder us. While first contact is all well and good, I imagine that alien death threats are pretty high up there on the list of things to not let the world know about. Society tearing itself apart in a wild panic is not condusive to Securing, Containing, or Protecting.

Personally, I think it's not even meant for us. But it doesn't matter what I think. It matters what the Foundation thinks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always figured the Foundation had contacted aliens of some sort or another a long time ago, because what kind of paranormal collectors would they be otherwise? Assuming that, this isn't first contact at all for them, and for some reason or another, they don't want it to be first contact for the rest of us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed, not a threat to normalcy and preventing first contact is just a dick move.

Also, witholding my vote until it's made more obvious. As it currently stands, I dun gid id.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is extraterrestrial communication not a threat to normalcy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not seeing what interest the Foundation has in keeping communications from extraterrestrials secret from the world, since it doesn't seem to be a threat to normallacy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The inspiration for this? I'm guessing David Brin's "Lungfish", a story about the remnants of several alien-built von Neumann probes that have gathered together around a base somewhere in the outer Solar system - a base which is being explored by a human ship (with one biological crew member, and one AI, which apparently strikes the probes as a unique solution to the crisis of the Makers; usually, either the AIs are slaves or they destroy their masters, but the humans have begun "raising" AIs to develop into independent sapience).

In the end, each of the probes is preparing to battle the others to carry out its own programming, to either welcome the newcomers, attack them, or warn them about the dangers among the stars…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh man, blowing up planets is a punishment now. Humans must be really screwy in the head according to some aliens.

Also, on further discussion in site19, it seems to just be killbots.

Meh. I am secure in my downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, I like the ambiguity. Interpretation is fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
by sentiant individuals

I believe that you probably meant to spell "sentient", but I suspect you might have wanted to actually use the word "sapient".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: <indecipherableAlienLanguage>ButIdon'twanttopressthebigredbutton!</indecipherableAlienLanguage>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enlighten us, guy? Or, try to make it more obvious? We're readers of the word, not the mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Telescopic observation of some of the nearer transmitters reveals them to be asteroid like bodies. Spectrograph analysis of these bodies reveals them to contain unusually high purity silicon and silicate based crystals, with phosphorous and boron based dopants.

Radio signals hidden by Solar background radiation? I can suspend disbelief for that assuming the technological status quo. But I'm skeptical that the above-quoted analysis is within our technological reach. I mean, Pluto 1,153 ± 10 km in radius, and we still only have the blurriest of photographs, which require considerable computer enhancement to look like anything beyond a blip of light. (See this picture of Pluto and Charon from the Hubble.) Just my opinion, but I'd recommend just saying there are a bunch of different points of origin out in the Kuiper Belt which transmit very slowly at low bit-rate.

Separate note - you might want to run over the article with a fine-tooth comb for spelling; I noticed a couple of errors (minor stuff, but noticeable).

Withholding my vote for now - I could go either way on this one, depending on subsequent revisions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Straw poll? Aliens are pulling straws, whoever gets the short straw gets to destroy earth?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fear I may have been too subtle in my implications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're taking a straw poll amongst themselves about whether to destroy us. Dun-dun-DAAH!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1923 did this better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
by sentiant individuals

Other than that, this doesn't seem to Wow me, or make me feel anything at all. As far as I can tell it's aliens that are telling us to do something, we don't know what. Is that it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did indeed mean discretely. Thank you for catching that.

I will take the other comment on board if others also find the signal description too lengthy. I went into it a bit, mainly for the benefit of those who are unfamiliar with the mechanics of radio-wave propagation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Discretely" and "discreetly" mean very different things; you want the former.

That said, I'm not sure you need that much detail regarding the mechanics of the signal and its interference. There isn't anything anomalous with regard to the signal, just its sources; it could just be described as a low-amplitude, low-frequency digital AM signal that easily gets drowned out by interference within the Solar System.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My fourth attempt at an SCP article. I'm trying to go for something subtle here, but I may have gone too far (one way or the other). Please bear the following in mind:

1. A cookie for he (or she) who recognises the inspiration for this.
2. I have not crunched any numbers on this, but I believe the science is roughly accurate. Let me know if I have erred.
3. This was written and posted while I was moderately inebriated. If it does not stand the harsh light of sobriety, then I will happily remove it.

Critique away!
